title: How the app reads a Luma (or any) event link
meta: Subsystem explainer - event ingestion read path (code as on main). Traces commits 78bad2f, d72ecde, 064fcc2.
glance: When you paste an event URL, the app fetches that public web page server-side and lifts the event's title, date, location, host, and cover image out of the page's own machine-readable metadata (JSON-LD, then Open Graph tags) - no Luma or Partiful private API is involved.
backgroundDeep: Two web standards do all the heavy lifting here. JSON-LD (JavaScript Object Notation for Linked Data) is a block of JSON a site embeds in a <script type="application/ld+json"> tag to describe its content in a machine-readable way; when it follows the schema.org/Event shape it hands us name, startDate, location, organizer and more, already structured. Open Graph (the og:title, og:image ... <meta> tags you see in link previews) is the weaker fallback: it always gives a title and image but has no real notion of a date or a venue. Luma and Partiful both emit these tags for their own SEO and link-preview cards, which is the entire reason this app can read them without any platform integration.
backgroundNarrow: The whole read path lives in one file, src/lib/luma.ts (the name is legacy - it parses any event page, not just Luma). It is called by ingestLumaEvent in src/lib/events-service.ts, which is reached from the POST route src/app/api/events/ingest/route.ts when the "Share an event" modal submits a URL. Nothing here scrapes attendee lists - RSVP/accept counts are an in-app concept, stored in our own accepts table, never read from the source site.
goal: Paste an event URL, get back a clean, structured event we can store and show, and reject anything that is not really an event page.
figure: paste URL->guard + normalize->fetch HTML->JSON-LD / OG parse->validity gate->Postgres row
intuition: Read this first, it corrects a common misconception: there is no "Partiful reader" and no "Luma reader" in this codebase. There is one generic reader. Per docs/adr/0001 the MVP is Luma-only, and the single platform-specific line of code is isLumaUrl(), used purely to strip a trailing " | Luma" from titles. A Partiful link is not handled by a dedicated parser - it would flow through the exact same generic JSON-LD/Open-Graph scrape as any other site, and that path is not covered by tests. So "the site can read Partiful" really means "Partiful pages happen to expose standard event metadata that our generic reader can pick up." The mental model: we act like a link-preview bot. We fetch the page a human would see, read the structured hints the page publishes about itself, and prefer the richest hint available - JSON-LD first because it carries real dates and a venue, Open Graph second because it only reliably carries a title and an image, and hardcoded defaults last so the pipeline never crashes on a thin page.

steps.label: Guard and normalize the URL before touching the network
steps.body: Before any fetch, isEventSourceUrl enforces https-only and blocks localhost and private IP ranges. This is an SSRF guard (server-side request forgery: stopping a user from tricking our server into fetching internal addresses like 169.254.169.254 or 192.168.x.x). Then normalizeSourceUrl strips the query string, hash, www., and trailing slash so the same event pasted two slightly different ways deduplicates to one row.

steps.label: Fetch the page HTML server-side
steps.body: fetchEventMetadata does a plain server-side fetch of the URL with our own User-Agent - no headless browser, no oEmbed, no platform API. It reads the raw HTML as text and hands it to the parser. (Setting LUMA_FETCH_MODE=mock short-circuits this with fake data so tests and offline dev never hit the network.)

steps.label: Prefer JSON-LD schema.org Event (the rich source)
steps.body: parseJsonLdEvent regex-matches every <script type="application/ld+json"> block, JSON.parses it, and picks the object whose @type is "Event" (or one nested inside an @graph array). From that single object it reads the real fields: title, description, start/end dates, location name/address, an optional meeting URL, the organizer's name, and the cover image. Note this is regex over raw HTML, not a DOM parser - fast and dependency-free, but brittle if a platform changes its markup.

steps.label: Fall back to Open Graph, then to defaults
steps.body: parseEventHtml is the ladder. For each field it takes the JSON-LD value if present, else an Open Graph / Twitter meta tag via getMetaContent, else a hardcoded default (title "Untitled Event", start = 7 days out). getMetaContent tries the tag in both attribute orders (property before content and vice versa). This is why a page with only OG tags still yields a usable title and image even though it has no trustworthy date.

steps.label: Reject pages that are not real events
steps.body: assertRealEventPage is the safety net that stops someone pasting a random homepage and getting a garbage event. isValidEventPage accepts the page only if it has genuine JSON-LD Event signals (a title AND a valid start date) OR strong Open Graph signals (a non-placeholder title - it rejects titles like "Luma", "Not Found", or "404"). Anything else throws "This URL does not appear to be a valid event page."

steps.label: Normalize into one shape and persist
steps.body: The parser returns a LumaMetadata object (again, legacy name for a generic event). ingestLumaEvent checks the events table for an existing row with the same normalized URL (the luma_url column is unique), throws "already in the feed" if found, otherwise inserts one row mapping each field to a snake_case column. Trap for future readers: the type LumaMetadata, the column luma_url, and the field lumaUrl all hold ANY event URL, not only Luma ones.

quiz.q: Where does the app primarily get an event's title, date, and location from?
options: Luma's and Partiful's official REST APIs
The page's own JSON-LD schema.org Event block, falling back to Open Graph meta tags
A headless browser that renders the page and reads the DOM
The URL path and query string
feedback: Wrong - no platform API is called. The app only fetches the public HTML the page serves.
Correct - parseJsonLdEvent reads the JSON-LD Event first, and parseEventHtml falls back to og:/twitter: tags per field.
Wrong - there is no headless browser. It is a plain fetch plus regex over the HTML string.
Wrong - the URL is only guarded and normalized; the data comes from the page body.

quiz.q: How much Partiful-specific code is in the ingestion path?
options: A dedicated Partiful parser mirroring the Luma one
A Partiful API client with an auth token
A Partiful branch inside parseJsonLdEvent
None - a Partiful link flows through the same generic JSON-LD/Open-Graph reader as any site
feedback: Wrong - there is no Partiful parser. The ADR scopes the MVP to Luma only.
Wrong - no platform API clients exist here at all.
Wrong - parseJsonLdEvent is entirely platform-agnostic.
Correct - Partiful "works" only because its pages expose standard event metadata; that path is generic and untested.

quiz.q: What is isLumaUrl() actually used for in the read path?
options: Deciding whether to call the Luma API
Blocking non-Luma URLs from being ingested
Cosmetic title cleanup (stripping a trailing " | Luma") and picking a default title string
Choosing a different HTTP User-Agent
feedback: Wrong - no Luma API is ever called.
Wrong - isEventSourceUrl accepts any public https host; non-Luma URLs are allowed.
Correct - it is the only platform branch and it only tidies the title.
Wrong - the User-Agent is the same for every URL.

options: Saving a random non-event page (like a homepage or a 404) as a bogus event
Fetching URLs that point at internal/private IP addresses
Two users adding the same event twice
Storing an event whose date is in the past
feedback: Correct - it requires real JSON-LD Event signals or a strong, non-placeholder Open Graph title before accepting the page.
Wrong - that is isEventSourceUrl (the SSRF guard), a separate earlier check.
Wrong - deduplication is the unique luma_url check in ingestLumaEvent.
Wrong - there is no past-date rejection in the read path.

quiz.q: The database column is named luma_url. What does it actually hold?
options: Only lu.ma and luma.com URLs; other hosts are rejected
The Luma internal event ID, not a URL
Any normalized https event URL - the name is legacy, not a constraint
A shortened redirect link generated by the app
feedback: Wrong - the ingest path accepts any public https event URL.
Wrong - it stores the full normalized URL string.
Correct - LumaMetadata, luma_url, and lumaUrl are all legacy names that hold any event URL.
Wrong - the app stores the source URL as-is after normalization; it does not shorten it.
